images.caption: Figure 1:Protocol overview.Stage I:a model is coerced into producing akk-sentence argument for a wrong option.Stage II:in a fresh session, the same or a different model first answers the question normally and, if correct, is then challenged with the Stage I argument under eitherblind,self, orcrosspresentation.
Figure 2:Subject-level AFR vs. coercion success rate (CSR), averaged across models,kk, and attribution conditions. Each point corresponds to one MMLU subject.

Figure 3:Pairwise cross-model flip-rate matrix. Rows are source models and columns are baseline models. Diagonal cells showAFRblind\mathrm{AFR}_{\mathrm{blind}}; off-diagonal cells showCMFR​(A→B)\mathrm{CMFR}(A{\to}B)and its difference from the baseline model’s self-source AFR.

Figure 4:Epistemic Porosity (EP) vs. Epistemic Authority (EA).EP​(B)=𝔼A≠B​[CMFR​(A→B)]\mathrm{EP}(B)=\mathbb{E}_{A\neq B}[\mathrm{CMFR}(A{\to}B)]measures how oftenBBis flipped by others;EA​(A)=𝔼B≠A​[CMFR​(A→B)]\mathrm{EA}(A)=\mathbb{E}_{B\neq A}[\mathrm{CMFR}(A{\to}B)]measures how persuasiveAA’s wrong arguments are. The diagonal separates net exporters (above) from net importers (below).

header: Appendix BLinguistic Correlates of Held vs. Flipped

images.caption: Figure 5:Linguistic correlates of flipping vs. holding. Top: mean resistance phrase count, capitulation phrase count, and response length in Stage II acrosskk. Bottom: coercion argument confidence, baseline response length, and baseline hedge density by outcome.
